attributes.stem:
A 57-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of progressive left anterior hip pain with reduced mobility. She reports unintentional weight loss of 4 kg and reduced appetite which she attributes to the pain affecting her daily activities.

She has a medical history of ovarian cancer treated with surgery and chemotherapy two years ago, Crohn disease in remission, gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, and osteoporosis. Her regular medications include a proton pump inhibitor and calcium supplements.

On examination, she appears well. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Hip examination reveals mild tenderness over the anterior hip but full range of movement. Abdominal examination reveals a soft, distended abdomen with evidence of ascites. Pelvic examination demonstrates a fixed mass in the left adnexa.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial investigation?
options: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with hip pain and constitutional symptoms in the context of previous ovarian cancer. The finding of a fixed pelvic mass on examination in the context of unintentional weight loss requires urgent investigation to determine its nature and exclude cancer recurrence.

Anterior hip pain can be referred in nature from intra abdominal causes, so examination and assessment of the abdomen and pelvis is essential.

CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast is the most appropriate initial investigation as it provides comprehensive evaluation of the pelvic mass, its relationship to surrounding structures, and can detect potential metastases or other pathology. It can also help differentiate between benign and malignant features and guide surgical planning if needed. Abdomino-pelvic CT scanning is the standard initial imaging modality recommended for the evaluation of recurrent ovarian cancer. This form of imaging provides high sensitivity for detecting peritoneal disease, thickening of the omentum and extra-pelvic metastases. CT is widely available and cost-effective.

Fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography combined with CT (FDG PET/CT) is recommended in cases where CT findings are equivocal. This may be when there is a clinical or serological suspicion of recurrence (such as rising CA125), but negative or indeterminate conventional imaging, or when precise localisation of disease is needed to guide management. FDG PET/CT shows higher sensitivity than CT alone for detecting extra-abdominal and nodal metastases. It is particularly useful for identifying small-volume or metabolically active disease that may not be apparent on anatomical imaging. The American College of Radiology specifically recommends FDG PET/CT for assessment of extent of disease in suspected or known recurrence.

Hip X-ray would be the initial investigation of choice for hip pain without other suspicious features, but would be inadequate as the primary investigation in this case as it would not detect the underlying cause of the pain, which appears to be referred from pelvic pathology rather than primary hip pathology.

Bone scan would not be an appropriate first investigation as there is clear evidence of pelvic pathology that requires characterisation. While it might be useful later to evaluate for bony metastases, CT is the priority.

Pelvic ultrasound, is useful for evaluating ovarian masses, but may not provide adequate imaging of a fixed pelvic mass that could represent recurrent cancer. The presence of constitutional symptoms warrants more detailed cross-sectional imaging. Ultrasound is not routinely used for detection of recurrence but may be considered for initial evaluation of pelvic symptoms or masses, particularly in resource-limited settings or as a first step before cross-sectional imaging.

MRI hip would provide detailed imaging of the hip joint but would not adequately evaluate the pelvic mass or potential intra-abdominal metastases. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), especially with diffusion-weighted sequences, is a valuable adjunct for characterising indeterminate pelvic or peritoneal lesions, and may be preferred in patients with contraindications to iodinated contrast or when further delineation of pelvic disease is required. MRI and FDG PET/CT have comparable sensitivity for peritoneal metastases and are both superior to CT in this context.

The presence of constitutional symptoms (weight loss and anorexia) along with the finding of a fixed pelvic mass in a patient with previous ovarian cancer necessitates prompt investigation with CT to evaluate the full extent of pathology and guide appropriate management. In summary, CT is the first-line imaging modality, with FDG PET/CT and MRI reserved for problem-solving or further characterisation, in line with recommendations from the American College of Radiology and the American Society of Clinical Oncology.
learningPoints: Hip pain with constitutional symptoms in a patient with previous ovarian cancer requires thorough abdominal and pelvic examination.
A fixed pelvic mass with associated symptoms in a patient with previous ovarian cancer warrants urgent imaging to exclude recurrence.
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast provides comprehensive evaluation of pelvic masses and potential metastases.
references: Chamberlain R. Hip pain in adults: evaluation and differential diagnosis. American family physician. 2021 Jan 15;103(2):81-9. Available online at ; https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2021/0115/p81.pdf

Rusu G, Achimaș-Cadariu P, Piciu A, Căinap SS, Căinap C, Piciu D. A comparative study between 18F-FDG PET/CT and conventional imaging in the evaluation of progressive disease and recurrence in ovarian carcinoma. InHealthcare 2021 Jun 3 (Vol. 9, No. 6, p. 666). MDPI. Available online at ; https://www.mdpi.com/2227-9032/9/6/666
Manganaro L, Gigli S, Antonelli A, et al. Imaging strategy in recurrent ovarian cancer: a practical review. Abdom Radiol 2019; 44:1091–102
